observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
1. Require the CGSO, in coordination with the concerned offices, to conduct a
complete physical inventory count of all inventories and prepare the RPCI
semestrally based on the results of the actual count with complete details
and description of the inventoried items;
2. Instruct the OCA and the CGSO to: (a) analyze the discrepancies noted and
reconcile their records; (b) effect the necessary adjustments in the books of
accounts, if warranted; and (c) maintain complete and updated SCs and
SLCs for each inventory item; and
3. Remind the concerned offices to submit the pertinent documents to the OCA
and the CGSO, immediately after the distribution of the inventory items; to
facilitate timely preparation of SSMI as a basis for recording corresponding
expense accounts; and to ensure to retain copies of said documents for
monitoring and recording purposes.

We likewise recommended that Management:
1. Require the IC to include all PPE items in the physical count for the OTC.
Thereafter, prepare and submit the updated working papers and complete
RPCPPE, including those that remained unlocated despite diligent efforts,
using the prescribed format under Annex 49 of the MNGAS for LGUs, for
evaluation of the AT and recommendation of the approval of the application
for cleansing to the proper authorities; and
2. Remind the CGSO to ensure that the RPCPPE contains all the required
information pertaining to each asset, especially the names of AOs, as
confirmed with the duly signed PAR and consistently observe the
assignment of a unique property number for each asset, to expedite the
reconciliation.

We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed to:
1. Require the IC to include all PPE items in the physical count for the OTC.
Thereafter, prepare and submit the updated working papers and complete
RPCPPE, including those that remained unlocated despite diligent efforts,
using the prescribed format under Annex 49 of the MNGAS for LGUs, for
evaluation of the AT and recommendation of the approval of the application
for cleansing to the proper authorities; and
2. Remind the CGSO to ensure that the RPCPPE contains all the required
information pertaining to each asset, especially the names of AOs, as
confirmed with the duly signed PAR and consistently observe the
assignment of a unique property number for each asset, to expedite the
reconciliation.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management:
a. Advise the LFC and other officials concerned to regularly monitor the CGS’
cash position and immediately notify the LCE of any cash insufficiency
noted;
b. Require the OCA and the CTO to ensure that monies held in trust are used
solely for their intended purpose;
c. Ensure that expenditures incurred are only for PPAs that are highly
necessary and cannot be dispensed with; and
d. Adopt effective revenue-enhancing strategies and cost-cutting measures to
eliminate the cash deficit/overdraft.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Remind the OCA to effect the necessary adjustments in the books of
accounts as soon as reconciling items are identified and verified in order to
reflect the correct balance of the cash account in the FS as at yearend; and
2. Instruct the OCA and the CTO to periodically reconcile their respective
books and cash records to avoid accumulation of discrepancies and ensure
that errors in both records are immediately detected and corrected.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the CTO and the OCA
to investigate the unreconciled balance of the CLT account. Thereafter, provide the
necessary course of action to ultimately present reconciled balances for both books.
We likewise recommended and the CTO concurred to:
1. Ensure the completeness and correctness of the entries recorded in the
cashbooks and practice daily updating and balancing of collections and
deposits in the cashbooks; and
2. Immediately transfer the amount received from the EDC to the appropriate
bank account intended for the SEF to facilitate the proper utilization of
fund.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Compel the concerned accountable officials and employees who are still
connected to the LGU to liquidate/settle their prior years’ CAs immediately,
either by liquidation, payment or through salary deductions;
2. Exert extra effort to locate the necessary SDs relative to the long outstanding
CAs and require those officials and employees who are already separated
from the service to settle/liquidate their outstanding obligations by sending
DLs. Copy of the said DLs bearing the receipts of the concerned accountable
officials/employees should be furnished the AT;
3. Conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts to ensure
that these are collected when these become due and demandable and that
CAs are liquidated within the prescribed period depending upon their
nature and purpose; and
4. Revisit the request for authority to write-off dormant CAs submitted to
COA and ensure compliance with the documentary requirements and forms
prescribed in COA Circular No. 2023-008 for CAs of former officials and
employees who are already deceased or cannot be located wherein the
settlement/collectability of the same is unlikely, and henceforth strictly
adhere to the governing law, rules and regulations on the grant, utilization
and liquidation of CAs.

We again recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Immediately remit the shares from its collections in the GF, to the concerned
NGAs upon receipt of the billing statements;
2. Coordinate with the NGAs to address the discrepancies between their billing
statements and the CGS’ records of actual collections;
3. Exercise due diligence by reviewing every detail of its transaction prior to
disbursement in order to prevent the incurrence of abnormal balances
resulting from excess payment/refund;
4. Direct the responsible Department/Office to expedite the implementation of
PPAs to maximize the readily available funds received from SAs within the
agreed timeframe, thus ensuring timely delivery of benefits to the intended
beneficiaries; and
5. Return the balances of the completed PPAs to the SAs or make
representations and obtain authority to utilize the unexpended balances for
purposes other than what are provided in the MOA.

We recommended and Management agreed to remind the OCA to refrain from
recording purchases of supplies and materials as outright expense except those
purchased out of the PCF which shall be for immediate use, and ensure that all
regular purchases shall be coursed through the inventory control account and
issuances thereof shall be recorded as they take place.

We recommended that Management encourage the CTO to strengthen security
controls over cash on hand by placing the vault in a restricted area with access limited
only to authorized personnel.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed to:
1. Require the recipient Barangays to immediately comply with the obligations
and commitments under the MOA to ensure accountability, transparency
and strict safeguarding of government funds from any misappropriation or
unnecessary use;
2. Consistently remind the CPDO, the CBO and the CEO to closely monitor
the implementation of PPAs under the 20% DF and ensure the preparation
and submission of complete and accurate project monitoring reports that
are relevant in decision-making; and
3. Adopt strategic measures on the implementation of the identified
development PPAs for the attainment of optimum utilization of the 20% DF
and the achievement of desired socio-economic development and
environmental management outcomes of the CGS, essential in the
realization of the constitutional objective of quality of life for all.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Require the recipient Barangays with insufficient LRs to immediately
submit the required documents in compliance with the requisites provided
by COA Circular 2012-001 and the MOA; and
2. Instruct the OCA to ensure that subsequent LRs of other Barangays shall
be strictly evaluated as to their completeness and validity before recording
in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Remind the CPDO and the CEO to exercise caution in the preparation of
their status and monitoring reports, especially those information submitted
to NGAs and other oversight bodies; and
2. Reconcile the inconsistencies noted between the reports of the CPDO and
the CEO, and the recipient Barangays. Thereafter, provide justifications
that the BDP assistance to the Barangays of Bulabog, Gimaloto and
Barayong, were not granted for projects that are already existing and
completed.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to
remind the CDRRMO to ensure timely implementation of priority PPAs and full
utilization of the LDRRMF, enhancing the LGU’s capability to manage all types of
emergencies and facilitate orderly transitions from disaster response to sustained
recovery in case of calamities.
We also recommended and Management agreed to review the balances of
continuing appropriations and consider the reversion of funds no longer needed only
after ensuring sufficient cash is available to cover the savings.

We recommended and Management agreed to remind the OCA to:
1. Ensure that the transfer of the remaining unspent DRRMF amounting to
₱431,822.44 in CY 2025 is properly recorded in the TF books by crediting
Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account to present the correct balance of the
account; and
2. Ensure that the succeeding transfers of the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-
MOOE to the STF are properly recorded as Due to Other Funds in the GF
books and simultaneously recognized both in the TF books at yearend.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Instruct the CDRRMO to prepare annually a proper and comprehensive
LDRRMFIP showing the important details on the planning and
implementation phases of the identified disaster-related PPAs funded by the
LDRRMF from all sources;
2. Require the CDRRMO and the OCA to ensure the correctness and
consistency of the information presented in the monthly Report on Sources
and Utilization of DRRMF; and
3. Direct the CDRRMO to submit the monthly Report on Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day of the succeeding month to
the AT as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and Management agreed to remind the CDRRMO to refrain
from charging expenditures not related to DRRM in the LDRRMF and ensure that all
disbursements are aligned with the specific objectives and are allowable expenses
under each PPA as reflected in the approved LDRRMP.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Resolve the existing issues or problems that were encountered in the
aforementioned LGSF-SBDP projects and consider allocating funds for the
construction of a concrete pavement or pathway, additional paint works,
and a drainage at the health centers of Barangays of Bogña, Santa Lucia,
and Sugod, Bacon District, respectively; and
2. Ensure that proper planning, regular monitoring and thorough inspection
are conducted by the CEO and end-user departments to avoid further issues
arising after project implementation and turnover, and to avoid idleness of
the projects of the CGS.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the SCLSB to:
1. Ensure proper planning, budgeting and implementation of the PPAs to
achieve efficient utilization of the SEF by strict monitoring and periodic
assessment;
2. Make certain that the budget reflects an equitable distribution of the funds
among the priority PPAs; and
3. Identify in the budget the specific names of schools to be repaired,
rehabilitated and improved to ensure the timely, effective and efficient
implementation and monitoring of the PPAs.

We recommended that Management require the SCLSB to provide
justifications on the charging of non-priority expenditures enumerated above.
We also recommended that hereafter, the SCLSB refrain from the practice of
charging non-priority expenditures to the SEF and strictly observe the prioritization
of expenses chargeable against the fund as provided under Section 272 of RA No. 7160
and Item No. 4.0 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01 s. 2017.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed to
remind the OCA to:
1. Facilitate the timely recording and set-up of taxes withheld among the GF,
the SEF and the TF books at yearend to immediately identify unrecorded
transactions; and exercise extra diligence in tracing the cause of the
abnormal monthly SL balances for the IWT, VAT, SEF and TF, and
prepare the necessary adjusting entries, if warranted;
2. Ensure that accurate amounts are transferred from the SEF and the TF
books to the GF books; and reconciled with the actual taxes withheld to
avoid the incurrence of abnormal balances among these funds; and
3. Require timely submission of payroll vouchers and their corresponding
attachments to facilitate timely recording of the income taxes withheld from
casuals and new employees to avoid negative balances.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed to:
1. Instruct the OCA to reconcile and immediately reflect the necessary
adjustments relative to the discrepancies noted between the accounting
records and the billing statements from the GSIS; and
2. Require the OCA to make the necessary representations with the GSIS and
concerned offices to exhaust all efforts to locate the relevant documents to
support the non-moving accounts, and thereafter, make the necessary
adjustments in the books, if needed.
We likewise recommended that the OCA ensure that adjustments to be
recorded are properly supported with complete documentations to prevent balances
from requiring further reconciliation.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations with modifications, and
Management agreed to strictly require the City Human Resource and Management
Office (CHRMO) and the OCA to:
1. Expedite the reconciliation between their records to come up with a
complete list of JO personnel with unremitted contributions to the HDMF
and classify those that are already separated from the CGS and/or with
unknown whereabouts. Thereafter, make representations with the HDMF
officials on the proper disposition of the unremitted contributions of those
members without MID numbers and seek their concurrence on the planned
refund; and
2. Demand from the concerned JO personnel who are still connected with the
CGS, the submission of their Pag-IBIG MID numbers to facilitate
immediate remittance of their contributions to the HDMF. Otherwise, their
salaries may be withheld pending the submission of the required
information.
We also recommended and the OCA concurred to investigate the cause of the
abnormal balance and effect the necessary adjustment, if warranted to arrive at the
correct account balance.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and Management agreed to
require the CHRMO to ensure that the deficient amounts on prior year’s contributions
of the concerned personnel be deducted from their succeeding salaries.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations with modifications and
Management agreed to:
1. Require the GFPS to fully comply with the provisions of COA Circular No.
2021-008 by using the created and assigned RC codes as additional reference
in its GPB and GAD AR;
2. Remind the OCA to maintain complete and updated SLs for the GFPS and
other GAD-related financial transactions; and
3. Instruct the CBO to indicate the RCs in the GAD Monitoring and Utilization
Report to facilitate ease of reconciliation and ensure a more integrated and
comprehensive accounting, monitoring and reporting of the GAD programs
and funds.
Provide complete SDs for the previously dropped real property units (RPUs) to establish the propriety of the adjustments made;

Analyze carefully and check the accuracy and completeness of the SDs, thereafter attach these to the JEV and furnish a copy to the AT for validation, in order to establish the accuracy and reliability of FS account balances;
Conduct immediate and proper validation of the untitled RPUs based on the report from CAO, considering the criteria provided under the IPSAS 17; and

Maintain Real Property Ledger Cards (RPLCs) with complete details as to its description, location, acquisition cost, additions, and disposition that will serve as SLs to support the balances reported in the FS.

Direct the OCA to provide the necessary adjusting entries on PPE to correct the recognition of semi-expendable properties and henceforth, consider appropriate account titles as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024; and
observations.managementAction: Provided necessary adjusting entries as per recommendations

observations.recommendation: Remind the OCA to ensure proper recording of semi-expendable properties and PPE items by strictly observing the prescribed threshold amount.
observations.managementAction: Ensured proper recording of semi-expendable PPE

observations.recommendation: We likewise recommended that Management capacitate the members of the LPMC to improve their monitoring and evaluation capability with the end view of producing more complete and accurate reports that are relevant in decision-making.
observations.managementAction: Capacitated the members of the LPMC

observations.recommendation: Strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and other applicable laws, rules and regulations governing the utilization of the LDRRMF.
Submit the corresponding LDRRMFIP of the aforesaid PPAs to the AT for verification purposes; and
observations.reason: Submitted the required report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the OCA and the CBO to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records, especially at year-end, to ensure the accuracy and correctness of the reported amounts on the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: Regular reconciliation is being made.

observations.recommendation: Resolution duly approved by two-thirds (2/3) of the total membership of the Local Peace and Order Council (LPOC) in support for the release and utilization of the Calamity Fund (CF);
Certification from the PNP Chief in the locality relative to the peace and order situation highlighting in concrete details the circumstances which require the urgency in allocating CF; and
observations.reason: The documents were submitted to the AT.

observations.recommendation: Physical and Financial Plan (Annex A of JC No. 2015-01) indicating the proposed proposed amount allocated for each PPA, where disbursements pertaining to CF shall be based.
observations.reason: The documents were submitted to the AT.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability;
Require the OCA and the CEO to submit immediately the required records and documents that will support the existence, ownership and condition of the unidentified infrastructures and validity of the JEVs without complete documentation; and
Require the OCA and the CGSO to prepare and maintain complete and updated records on PPE.
Likewise, we recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
Make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts based on the deficiencies noted in audit and submit the JEVs together with complete SDs to the AT for verification;
As far as practicable, classify PPE accounts acquired in lump sum amount based on the Chart of Accounts provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009 in order to properly account and fairly present these in the FS; and
Analyze carefully and review transactions and attach the complete SDs to the JEV before taking them up in the books of accounts in order to establish the accuracy and reliability of FS account balances.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to require the City Accountant and the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) - City Engineer to reconcile their records as to the status and costs incurred related to ongoing projects of the CGS.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to prioritize the reconciliation of the Cash in Bank accounts to come up with the correct balances in the FS; and
Facilitate the closure of unnecessary bank accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-382, Section 29 (3) Article VI of the 1987 Philippine Constitution and other relevant laws, rules and regulations.
Likewise, we recommended and the OCA agreed to:
Ensure that the submitted BRS for all bank accounts of the CGS are supported with the BS, DMs/CMs and other supporting schedules/documents;
Refrain from making JEVs for transactions which have not yet occurred;
Revert the entries made for stale checks; and
Take up all valid reconciling items by preparing JEVs duly supported with proper documentation and furnish the AT with copies of the same.
Further, we recommended and the CGS agreed to monitor the status of outstanding checks to prevent spoilage, in accordance with Section 59 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation and Management agreed to require the CGSO to conduct physical inventory and inspection of unserviceable properties to account for all unserviceable and obsolete items so that these will be properly disposed and dropped from the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Evaluate thoroughly the proposed priority development projects to be funded under the BDP as to their readiness for implementation or procurement to avoid delays or non-implementation thereof;
Define clearly in the MOA the period of liquidation of CFA and develop a sound incentive mechanism to promote and encourage prompt liquidation by the barangays; and
Strengthen coordination between or among offices and ensure the completeness and accuracy of reports produced to provide relevant information to decision makers.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Enjoin the LDRRMO to exert more effort in implementing actions and measures pertaining to all aspects of disaster risk reduction and management towards building a disaster-resilient LGU and communities;
Monitor closely the implementation of all PPAs under the LDRRMF to facilitate corrective action, if any, and the timely reprogramming and reallocation of fund for optimum utilization; and
Devise a plan or mechanism in prompt utilization of future donations from other sources to have more latitude in providing additional aid to intended beneficiaries.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
Direct the BAC, the Secretariat and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to abide strictly by the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR and other relevant issuances on government procurement;
Submit within five working days upon execution or issuance of all copies of perfected contracts together with the SDs as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
Instruct the City Accountant and the OIC – City Engineer to observe at all times the requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-001 on the procurement/ implementation of infrastructure projects to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit promptly the required liquidation documents to the SA to minimize or eliminate the unreconciled amount of the Due to NGAs account.
We likewise recommended and the OCA agreed to exert utmost effort in retrieving the necessary documents to determine the nature of idle funds, and consequently, prepare the necessary adjusting entries and furnish the AT copies of JEVs supported with complete documentation.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the BAC and other concerned officials to:
Adhere to the guidelines in determining the cost of consultancy as provided in the Manual of Procedures on the Procurement of Consulting Services;
Ensure that all pertinent information are indicated in the bidding documents and other procurement reports; and
Observe the bidding procedures in the procurement of consulting services.

We recommended and Management agreed to comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and 2012-001 in granting financial assistance to NGOs/POs to avoid the risk of loss and wastage of government funds.

We recommended and Management agreed that the concerned offices,  PGSO
and PAccO:


     a. Prioritize and finish the reconciliation process, and  ensure  that
        discrepancies noted  in  the  reconciliation  of  PPE  records  are
        investigated immediately and adjusted in the books accordingly;


     b. Comply strictly with the guidelines and procedures for the one-time
        cleansing of PPE accounts of the PGS to establish PPE balances that
        are verifiable as to existence,  condition  and  accountability  as
        provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006; and


     c. Provide/Furnish the PAccO with the inspection and appraisal reports
        of the demolished buildings as basis for derecognition thereof from
        the books of accounts.  We suggested that the PAccO  use  secondary
        or other documentary  evidences  to  determine  the  costs  of  the
        demolished buildings.

       Likewise,  we  also  recommended  and  Management  agreed  that  the
Provincial Accountant (PA) require the concerned PAccO personnel to provide
a separate column for depreciation expense recognized for the year  in  the
Schedule of Depreciation.

Acting PT coordinate with the concerned AGDBs  to  obtain  copies  of  prior
period CMs and DMs identified in the BRS  or  trace  the  said  transactions
from PTO records.

review and comply strictly with the requirements set forth in  the  MOA  and

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the BAC to:

We recommended and Management agreed that the:


     a. Concerned Department Heads seek formally the approval of the Source

        purpose.  Otherwise, instruct the PTO to return immediately to  the

     b. Concerned offices to facilitate the timely disbursement  of  future
        fund transfers intended as calamity aids and for essential needs of
        the constituents, especially during the pandemic; and


     c. PTO to notify immediately  the  concerned  offices  whenever  trust
        funds are received for PGS implementation.


      We also reiterated our  prior  year’s  recommendation  and  Management
agreed that the PTO and PAccO work closely together to  identify  the  dates
and amounts of fund transfers  received  to  avoid  gaps  in  the  dates  of
receipt of funds and their recognition in the books of accounts.

a. Coordinate with the barangay officials and the community to  ensure

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct  the  BAC  Chairman
to instruct the:

     a.  BAC-TWG  to  include  during  post-qualification   a   comparative
        evaluation showing the number of projects with the  same  equipment
        and  key  personnel  pledged  and  assigned  by  the  same   lowest

        overlapping implementation period within the Province of  Sorsogon;
        and


     b. BAC to consider and recommend to the HOPE to formulate policies  in

         • The sufficiency and availability of  equipment  pledged  by  the
           contractors with regard to projects  simultaneously  awarded  to

           and


         • Setting the limit for the number of  contracts  handled  by  key
           personnel  to  ensure  reasonable  performance  of  duties   and
           functions, without  affecting  the  quality  and  timeliness  of
           project completion;


     c. BAC-TWG  to  disqualify  future  bids  wherein  key  personnel  and
        equipment are  already  pledged/used  in  other  ongoing  projects,
        including  Contractors  whose  certified  List   of   All   Ongoing
        Government and Private Contracts document reflected several ongoing
        projects with actual completion of  15 per  cent  as  evaluated  to
        avoid overlapping projects; and


     d. BAC-TWG to verify/validate the  data/information  reported  by  the
        Contractor on the list of all ongoing projects with the PEO Project
        Status Report as of certain date.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the  concerned  Department  Heads
to:

     a. Define clearly all future infrastructure  projects  in  the  AB/SB,
        which shall be aligned with AIP/SIP, Provincial PDIP, and PDPFP/CDP
        and included in the APP/SPP; and


     b. Comply strictly with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated
        November 4, 2020  in  the  determination  of  priority  development
        projects chargeable against the 20% DF.

We recommended and Management agreed that the PAccO and the PTO:

     a. Reconcile the difference between the balances of the loans  payable
        to LBP with the bank records;


     b. Reconcile the difference between the balances of both the PAccO and
        the PTO on the loans payable to LOGOFIND through confirmation  with
        the said financial institution; and


     c.   Comply  strictly  with  the  pertinent  provisions  of  IPSAS  in
        recording transactions.

We recommended and Management agreed that  all  concerned  Department
Heads and personnel submit the report  on  the  distribution,  consumption,
issuance and utilization of welfare goods,  drugs  and  medicines,  medical
supplies and other supplies related to COVID-19  programs  and  activities,

We  reiterated  our  previous  year’s  audit   recommendations   that
Management require the PPMIU to:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management: 

Provide sufficient manpower, facilities and materials needed by the concerned offices in order to properly account for the acquisition and disposition of inventories;

Require the Inventory Committee to exercise diligence in performing its functions and responsibilities as required in EO No. 24, Series of 2019, as well as the pertinent provisions of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs, Volume I;

Observe proper planning and budgeting procedures in order to come up with Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) and APP that are reflective of the actual supplies and materials requirement of the CGS;

Require the Inventory Committee and the OCA to prioritize the reconciliation between the Inventories accounts balances per books and per RPCI; and

Require the CGSO and the OCA to properly prepare and maintain complete and updated Stock Cards (SC) and Supplies Ledger Cards (SLC), respectively, and reconcile their records regularly. 

Furthermore, we recommended that the City Accountant:

Carefully analyze the transactions before taking them up in the books of accounts in order to properly account for the acquisition and issuance of the supplies and materials inventory based on International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 12, Inventories;

Make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of the affected inventory and expense accounts; and 

Submit the necessary supporting documents as basis for the adjustment amounting to ₱200,000.00, otherwise, revert the adjustment made.

Further, we recommended that the CGSO:

Prepare and use the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) in accordance with Sections 41 and 121 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Annex 33 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II; and
Come up with internal control procedures that will ensure proper handling of inventories to prevent or minimize the risk of loss and misuse such as maintenance of logbook for expected and actual deliveries based on approved POs and Delivery Receipts and making use of Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for issuance of small tangible items with serviceable life of more than one year but small enough to be considered as PPE.

Furthermore, we recommended that the BAC Secretariat:

Prepare the supplemental APP based on supplemental PPMPs and submit the same to the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) and to COA in accordance with Section 7.4 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA No. 9184; and

Immediately furnish the CGSO with copies of approved POs for monitoring purposes of the said office.

Institute appropriate criminal or administrative sanctions for the continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, if warranted.

We recommended that Management require:

The Local Finance Committee:

Conduct a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue, taking into account prior years’ experience and statistics as aids in setting targets;

Review the present budgeting to set a clearer guide for the concerned revenue generating departments towards attaining the targets set; and

Provide for the targets on accounts that did not have projections which should be based on statistics, prior years’ experience and future expectations.

The CTO and other departments involved in generating revenues endeavor to perform efficiently in the revenue administration by considering, among others, the following:

Conduct a periodic review to ascertain whether collection targets are attained and in case of shortfall in collections, to analyze the reasons and to look into alternative actions to avoid incurrence of lapses in projection in the future; and

Intensify further the collection of business and RPT and avail of administrative and judicial remedies in enforcing payments particularly in tax delinquency.

We recommended that the CTO perform examination of the books of accounts of business establishments and/or exhaust possible means in obtaining the correct gross receipts/sales for the preceding calendar year thru the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) or the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Further, we recommended that the BPLO and the CTO:

Require the taxpayer to present and submit the Income Tax Return (ITR) for the preceding CY, or if not yet available, the ITR for the next preceding CY, together with the other financial documents such as Audited FS and proof of payment, to be retained by both offices in the taxpayer’s permanent file for current and future reference; and

Require the submission of the BIR Certificate of Registration or BIR Form 2303 for all businesses applying or renewing business permits to validate the correctness of the line of business indicated in their application forms and to avoid misapplication of tax rates.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Register the 208 RPUs with market value amounting to ₱685.92 million under the GF and land worth ₱72,000 under the SEF under the Torrens Title System as proof of absolute ownership of the City Government thereto and in order to safeguard the assets against third party claims; and
Thereafter, seek authority from the SP for the City Mayor to donate the 30 RPUs being used by various schools and facilitate the subsequent transfer to them, if applicable.
We also reiterated our prior years’ recommendation and Management agreed to allocate adequate budget and utilize it for the titling of the subject land properties in the name of the City Government to establish absolute ownership and reduce the risk of third party claims.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations and Management agreed to:
Instruct the City Accountant to continue and strengthen coordination with the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) - City Engineer and CGSO to determine the condition of the infrastructure assets constructed in CYs 2015 and below, in order to assess the carrying amounts of the said assets and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to arrive at a more reliable Infrastructure Asset account balances in the FS;
Require the OIC - City Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of RPCLRN; and
Direct the Inventory Committee and CGSO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation and Management agreed to require the CGSO to conduct physical inventory and inspection of unserviceable properties so that they may be properly disposed of and dropped from the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386.
We also recommended and Management agreed to strictly observe the requirements of COA Circular No. 95-006 on the prompt submission/furnishing to the Audit Team of report of inspection and schedule or notice of condemnation/destruction of government property and other disposable assets, to enable the latter to perform its duties relating to the post-audit of the transaction at the most appropriate time and manner.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the City Accountant to properly prepare and maintain the SLs for CIP accounts in order to produce a more reliable and readily available information regarding the projects recorded under the CIP accounts and to reflect them accurately and reliably in the FS; and
Require the City Accountant and the OIC - City Engineer to reconcile their records as to the status and costs incurred related to ongoing projects of the City Government.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Provide sufficient manpower, facilities and materials needed by the concerned offices in order to properly account for the acquisition and disposition of inventories;
Require the Inventory Committee to exercise diligence in performing its functions and responsibilities as required in EO No. 24, Series of 2019, as well as the pertinent provisions of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
Observe proper planning and budgeting procedures in order to come up with PPMP and APP that are reflective of the actual supplies and materials requirement of the City Government;
Require the Inventory Committee and the Office of the City Accountant to prioritize the reconciliation between the Inventories accounts balances per books and per RPCI; and
Require the CGSO and the Office of the City Accountant to properly prepare and maintain complete and updated SCs and SLCs, respectively, and reconcile their records regularly.
We also recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
Carefully analyze the transactions before taking them up in the books of accounts in order to properly account for the acquisition and issuance of the supplies and materials inventory based on IPSAS 12, Inventories;
Make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of the affected inventory and expense accounts; and
Submit the necessary supporting documents as basis for the adjustment amounting to ₱200,000, otherwise, revert the adjustment made.
Further, we recommended and the CGSO agreed to:
Prepare and use the RIS and SSMI in accordance with Sections 41 and 121 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Annex 33 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II; and
Come up with internal control procedures that will ensure proper handling of inventories to prevent or minimize the risk of loss and misuse such as maintenance of logbook for expected and actual deliveries based on approved POs and DRs and making use of ICS for issuance of small tangible items with serviceable life of more than one year but small enough to be considered as PPE.
Furthermore, we recommended and the BAC Secretariat agreed to:
Prepare the supplemental APP based on supplemental PPMPs and submit the same to the GPPB and to COA in accordance with Section 7.4 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Immediately furnish the CGSO with copies of approved POs for monitoring purposes of the said office.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
Submit the necessary supporting documents in order to provide sufficient basis in taking up the adjustments in the books amounting to ₱69.95 million; and
Observe due diligence in recording transactions by using the proper accounts as provided under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 and ensuring sufficient basis and documentation in order to come up with accurate and faithfully represented FS.
We also recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to:
Exclude the advance payments received from taxpayers in the preparation of the Certified List of Taxpayers in order to provide the Office of the City Accountant an accurate basis for the set-up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year; and
Prepare and submit the Certified List of Delinquent Taxpayers as of December 31, 2020, in order to support the accuracy and reliability of the RPT and SET Receivable accounts presented in the FS.
Further, we recommended and the OIC - City Assessor agreed to, as practicable as possible, furnish the CTO with the Assessment Roll and General Statistics generated on the same dates, on or before December 31 of each year in order to enable the latter to prepare a more accurate and reliable basis in the preparation of the Certified List of Taxpayers.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure compliance with the documentary requirements for all disbursements chargeable against the 20 per cent Development Fund.
Further, we reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation and Management agreed to strictly comply with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1, by taking into consideration the following:
Develop a concrete plan that will articulate the PPA, timeline, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.; and
Adopt strategies to maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We recommended and the LSB agreed to strictly comply with the requirements of Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and DepEd, DBM and DILG JC Nos. 1 s. 2017, 1 and 2 s. 2020 in identifying the PPAs that reflect an equitable distribution of the mandated priority expenses and refrain from using the SEF outside the scope for which it was established to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in audit.
We recommended and the LSB agreed to ensure adherence to the pertinent rules and regulations for the efficient and economic use of its resources to safeguard the fund from loss or wastage.

We recommended that Management:

Instruct the City Accountant to continue and strengthen coordination with the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) – City Engineer to determine the condition of the infrastructure assets constructed in CYs 2015 and below, in order to assess the carrying amounts of the said assets and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to arrive at a more reliable Infrastructure Asset account balances in the FS;

Require the OIC - City Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network (RPCLRN); and

Direct the Inventory Committee and City General Services Office (CGSO) to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and Report of Local Road Network (RLRN), respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We also recommended that the City Accountant and the CGSO prepare and maintain separate subsidiary records/Local Road Network Ledger Cards (LRNLCs) and Local Road Network Property Cards (LRNPCs), respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Instruct the City Accountant to continue and strengthen coordination with the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) - City Engineer to determine the condition of the infrastructure assets constructed in CYs 2015 and below, in order to assess the carrying amounts of the said assets and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to arrive at a more reliable Infrastructure Asset account balances in the financial statements (FS);
Require the OIC - City Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of RPCLRN; and
Direct the Inventory Committee and CGSO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008.
We also recommended and the City Accountant and the CGSO agreed to prepare and maintain separate subsidiary records/LRNLCs and LRNPCs, respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations and Management agreed to:
Require the CGSO and the City Accounting Office to prioritize the reconciliation process and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly;
Require the City Accounting Office to maintain complete and updated PPELCs to support GL account balances and the CGSO to keep corresponding PCs for control purposes; and
Require the CGSO to conduct physical inventory and inspection of all unserviceable and obsolete items so that they may be properly disposed of and dropped from the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386.
Further, we recommended and the City Accounting Office agreed to continue its efforts in tracing back prior years’ acquisitions to have complete details of the PPE accounts that will serve as basis in the computation of depreciation, in order to present true value of the depreciable PPE in the FS.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations and Management agreed to:
Analyze the transactions affected, investigate the cause/s of the discrepancies noted, prepare the necessary adjusting entries and furnish the Audit Team copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers with complete supporting documents; and
Conduct periodic reconciliation of its records with the SAs to arrive at a correct/reconciled balance of the Due to NGAs account.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the City Accountant and the Acting City Treasurer to resolve the discrepancy of ₱3.69 million and come up with reconciled balances of the Cash in Bank accounts;
Direct the City Accountant to conduct a thorough review of records to determine the validity of long outstanding DMs amounting to ₱1.30 million and make the necessary adjustments; and
Instruct the City Accountant and the Acting City Treasurer to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records so that adjustments can be promptly effected, thus ensuring fair presentation of Cash in Bank accounts in the FS.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and 2012-001 in granting financial assistance to NGOs/POs to minimize or to avoid the risk of loss and wastage of government funds;
In the future, instruct the concerned officials to ensure that all salient provisions are incorporated in the MOA for all undertakings for an effective and efficient implementation of the same, taking into consideration the deficiencies noted in audit;
In the meantime, require the liquidation of financial assistance granted to the NGOs/POs under the TF by demanding submission of liquidation reports duly signed by the officers of the NGOs/POs and supported with complete documentation per COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
Thereafter, stop the practice of granting financial assistance to NGOs/POs from the TF in order to accumulate the said funds for the subsequent transfer to the GF.
We also recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
Make an adjusting entry to transfer the amounts recorded under the Trust Liabilities account to the Due from NGOs/POs account under the GF once the amounts transferred to various NGOs/POs are liquidated or returned; and
Analyze thoroughly the transactions to arrive at the proper treatment of the same before recording them in the books.

We recommended and the CDC, in coordination with the LFC, agreed to update regularly the LDIP to ensure inclusion of all development PPAs funded from the 20 per cent Development Fund which are aligned with the priorities of the present administration.
Further, we reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation and Management agreed to strictly comply with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1, by taking into consideration the following:
Develop a concrete plan that will articulate the PPA, timeline, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.;
Adopt strategies to maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all; and
Strengthen the management of reporting information by improving on its monitoring and evaluation capabilities and coordination among offices with the end view of producing more complete and accurate reports that are relevant in decision-making.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the liquidation of financial assistance granted to the NGOs/POs by demanding submission of liquidation reports duly signed by the officers of the NGOs/POs and supported with complete documentation per COA Circular No. 2012-001;
Demand the return of unexpended balances of financial assistance if the project has already been completed/fully implemented; or refund if unutilized or has not been used in accordance with the MOA; and
Request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts from NGOs/POs in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Expedite compliance of all the conditions and requirements provided under COA Circular No.  2016-005 to facilitate the approval of pending request for write-off of dormant receivable accounts; and
Conduct regular and periodic monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts to ensure that these are collected when these become due and demandable.
We also recommended and the City Accountant agreed to consider setting up in the books of accounts provision for impairment for possible losses in accordance with the applicable IPSAS and COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit all documents as required by COA Circular No. 2016-005 to support the request for authority to write-off dormant cash advances; and
Increase the bonding requirements of AOs with insufficient bond, pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the City Government, otherwise ensure that cash advance granted is within the limit of the fidelity bond.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the BAC, the Secretariat and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to strictly abide by the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR and other relevant issuances on government procurement;
Remind them also to exercise due diligence in the performance of their duties to ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully adhered to; and
Require all members of the BAC, the Secretariat and the TWG to undergo relevant trainings on government procurement that will enhance their capabilities in discharging their respective functions in an effective and efficient manner.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the OIC - City Engineer to:
Facilitate the completion of delayed infrastructure projects through constant monitoring and coordination with the concerned contractors; and
Validate the work accomplishment on all infrastructure projects as reflected in the contractors’ SWA in order to ensure that payments made are commensurate to the actual work accomplished.
For future procurement of infrastructure projects, we also recommended and the OIC - City Engineer agreed to carefully carry out a feasibility/preliminary engineering study and detailed engineering to ensure that issues such as technical viability of the project, road right of way, relocation of informal settlers and coordination with other government agencies relative to the issuance of required permits/clearances, are properly addressed to ensure smooth implementation of projects.

We recommended and Management agreed to facilitate the conduct of general revision of real property assessments, which shall commence upon the enactment of the SFMV into an ordinance by the SP, in compliance with Sections 212 and 219 of RA No. 7160, with the end view of achieving the target set on RPT collection.

Require the submission of the BIR Certificate of Registration or BIR Form 2303 for all businesses applying or renewing business permits to validate the correctness of the line of business indicated in their application forms and to avoid misapplication of tax rates; and
Hold the person/s responsible and accountable in the verification of documents and in the assessment and computation of business tax for whatever errors that may be found in audit.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Organize an ECMC that will be tasked to prepare the ECM Plan for the City of Sorsogon and coordinate the delivery of necessary assistance and services to the evacuees; and
Allocate funds in its LDRRM budget for the construction of additional evacuation centers to address the shortage.
We likewise recommended that the CEO observe pertinent provisions of DepEd-DSWD-DILG-DOH JMC No. 1, Series of 2013, in the design and construction of evacuation centers.

We recommended and Management agreed to re-evaluate the non-moving unexpended balances of fund transfers received from various NGAs and thereafter:
Return the balances to the NGAs if still identifiable or make representations and obtain authority from them to utilize the unexpended balances for purposes other than what are provided in the agreements; and
Request for write-off of balances which remained non-moving for 10 years or more and where settlement could no longer be ascertained, in accordance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management
require the OIC-City Accountant and City Treasurer to exert utmost effort to
reconcile the accounting records with the cashbook on a regular basis to support the
correctness of the Cash in Bank balance in the financial statements.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendations that the City
Government:

a. require the concerned officers and employees to liquidate/settle
immediately their outstanding cash advances by demanding the
immediate submission of duly accomplished liquidation reports.
Otherwise, withhold any money due them;

b. strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure
timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of
balances of cash advances by monitoring strictly the period of liquidating
cash advances;

c. exert extra effort to locate the whereabouts of separated accountable
officers and issue final demand letters; and

d. comply strictly with the provision of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash
advances.

We likewise recommended that Management require the OIC-City
Accountant to trace the entries made that resulted in the negative balances of the
cash advance balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries in the agency books
in order to present the correct amount of the Cash Advances accounts in the
financial statements; and make appropriate action to those who did not liquidate
their cash advances within the prescribed period or render account of their
agcountabilities after demand pursuant to CSC Memorandum Circular No. 12,5.
2012.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that Management require the
CGSO to facilitate the immediate disposal of the aforementioned unserviceable
properties and waste materials to recoup any salvage value thereof. Specifically,
we recommended the following measures:

a. conduct an appraisal of the unserviceable properties and waste materials and
furnish the auditor a copy of the appraisal report thereof;

b. dispose the unserviceable properties based on the most appropriate mode
of disposal pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that Management:

a. enroll the Job Orders in the biometric system to properly monitor their
attendance during working hours and avoid circumvention;

b. require the CHRMO to formulate a plan and issue uniform policy
guidelines on the hiring of Job Order personnel to strengthen internal
controls pertaining thereto;

c. reduce/limit hiring of Job Orders and fill up vacant positions, evaluate
and determine the appropriate personnel complement of each office and
effect amendments in their respective plantilla, if warranted;

d. indicate in the Job Order contract the specific office, barangay or street
(if applicable) where the JO will be detailed/deployed to properly track the
movement of JO personnel; and

e. submit Accomplishment Reports detailing actual works done for the
month and not just copy what is stated in their job descriptions.

As a control measure, we likewise recommended that Management require
the CHRMO to prepare the Human Resources Plan every year for determination
and evaluation of the necessity of the personnel hired and to be hired during the
period.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management
require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) or the concerned officials to submit
infrastructure contracts within five (5) days from execution thereof, duly supported
with all the documents required under the Revised Implementing Rules and
Regulations of RA 9184 in order to conduct timely and judicious review and
evaluation thereof. We likewise recommended that Management require the BAC
and the City Engineer to comply with the above-mentioned law, rules and
regulations to protect the interest of the government and to prevent unwarranted
disbursements of public funds.

For succeeding projects, we recommended that Management require the City
Accountant to fully withhold the 10 per cent retention money base on the total
amount due to the contractor prior to any deduction and shall be retained every
progress payment until fifty percent (50%) of the total value of works in
accordance with the provisions of the above cited rules and regulations, to
sufficiently cover any uncorrected discovered defects or third party liabilities in
the implementation of infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee prepare the Project
Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB in the prescribed format
within 14 days after the end of every semesters as required under Section 12.2 of the
RIRR to allow monitoring by appropriate government oversight body of
compliance with the provisions of the GPPB and ensure transparency on government
procurement. Likewise, we suggested that the Office of the Auditor be furnished with
a printed copy of the said monitoring report.

‘We recommended that Management require the OIC-City Accountant to
conduct a thorough review of the completeness of supporting documents before
effecting payment on claims. We further recommended that the concerned
responsible City officials submit the relevant required documents, otherwise, we
shall be constrained to suspend the payments to contractors.

In line with the noted deficiencies, we recommended that Management
require the BAC and Office of the City Engineer to strictly comply with provisions
of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulation of Republic Act No. 9184 to
avoid suspension/disallowance in the post-audit of these transactions.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of charging to
LDRRMF expenses which are not related to and do not support disaster risk
management activities. We likewise recommended that the City Officials adhere
strictly to the guidelines set under Section 21 of RA 10121 and COA Circular No.
2012-02 in the appropriation and utilization of LDRRMF.

‘We recommended that Management require the:

a. CDRRMO to stop the incurrence of excessive catering services; and

b. accountable/responsible officers or employees to refund the excess
payment made to supplier totaling $45,000.00.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of charging to the
Special Education Fund expenses which are not priority and do not fall within the
purpose for which the fund was created. We likewise recommended that the City
Officials adhere strictly to the guidelines set forth under Section 272 of RA 7160,
DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01 dated April 14, 1998, DepEd-DBM-DILG
Joint Circular No. 01-A dated March 14, 2000 and 01-B dated June 25, 2001 in the
utilization of the Special Education Fund.

‘We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations for Management
to:

a. comply with the amended schedule to be observed in GAD Planning and
Budgeting specified in JMC No. 2016-01 particularly on the submission of
GPBs and GAD ARs to the DILG and PCW; and

b. require the GFPS to prepare the GPB and GAD AR following the
revised format provided under the new Annexes D and E of JMC No.
2016-01.

We recommended that Management implement the Pili Festival
economically in order to reduce expenses and to avoid depletion of funds for other
projects, programs and activities. Likewise, Management shall exercise due
prudence to protect and conserve government funds and that unnecessary and
extravagant expenditures are prevented.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officialls to
monitor the implementation of the projects and programs under the 20 per cent
Development Fund and ensure its optimal utilization. We likewise recommended
that the City Planning and Development Office incorporate in the AIP the detailed
or complete description of the projects to be undertaken under the said fund.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation  that Management require the CGSO to facilitate the immediate disposal of the aforementioned unserviceable properties and waste materials to recoup any salvage value thereof.

We likewise recommended that management require the:

a. CGSO to file an application for disposal of unserviceable properties totaling P31,167,777.64 together with the duly accomplished IIRUP and appraised properties for validation and inspection of the COA Technical Audit Specialist; and

b. OIC-City Accountant to exert extra effort in reconciling the Other PPE account to accurately determine the composition of the P31,167,777.64 deemed as unserviceable properties and disclose the status/condition of such properties in the Notes to Financial Statements.
